message:
harden(codec): saturate InputSerial::elapsed_millis against future-dated serials + beads status sync

`InputSerial::elapsed_millis` computed `now.0 - self.0` directly on the
raw u64 millis value. That subtraction panics in debug builds (and
wraps in release) whenever `self.0 > now.0`. The naive contract — "the
serial is always in the past" — breaks in two real scenarios we have
already seen in the field:

1. **Remote host clock skew.** `InputSerial::from(SystemTime)` derives
   its value from `SystemTime::now()` on the producer side. When a
   frankenterm client is talking to a mux server or another host whose
   wall clock is ahead of ours (NTP drift, hibernate resume, fresh VM
   with no clock source yet), the serial we receive can legitimately be
   a few seconds into our local future. A debug-mode panic is a hard
   failure for what is effectively "the remote clock is a little fast".
2. **Adversarial or fuzzed payloads.** A codec fuzzer or a malicious
   peer can trivially hand us a serial deep in the future. We should
   not crash the consumer on parsed input.

Switch to `now.0.saturating_sub(self.0)` so a future-dated serial just
reports zero elapsed milliseconds and the caller sees "this event
happened at or after now" rather than a panic. Add a regression test
(`input_serial_elapsed_millis_saturates_when_serial_in_future`) that
constructs a serial 24 hours in the future via
`SystemTime::now() + Duration::from_secs(60 * 60 * 24)` and asserts the
returned elapsed is exactly 0.

Also sync .beads/issues.jsonl: three issue status fields updated (no
insertions or deletions; line count unchanged at 1717) reflecting
routine bead state transitions from other agents working in parallel.

 {"id":"ft-12e8l","title":"[SEV] mux-server Alert-after-PaneRemoved re-inserts HashMap entry, permanent leak","description":"crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl/src/dispatch.rs:256 processes MuxNotification::Alert by calling handler.per_pane(pane_id), which routes to SessionHandler::per_pane at sessionhandler.rs:282. That function uses HashMap::entry(..).or_insert_with(..) — a get-or-insert. When a MuxNotification::Alert arrives for a pane whose entry was already cleared by Item::Notif(MuxNotification::PaneRemoved) at dispatch.rs:247, the or_insert_with re-creates a fresh PerPane entry. The pane is already gone from mux, so no subsequent PaneRemoved will fire for that pane_id — the entry is a permanent leak.

Concrete interleaving:
  1. Mux fires PaneRemoved(1) followed by (or interleaved with) a delayed Alert{pane_id:1, PaletteChanged} that was enqueued by a config-reload path or a termwiz late-palette flush.
  2. Dispatch loop processes PaneRemoved -> handler.remove_per_pane(1) clears the map entry (line 247).
  3. Dispatch loop processes Alert -> handler.per_pane(1) -> or_insert_with -> re-creates an entry (line 256).
  4. Line 262 schedule_pane_push(1) likewise calls per_pane() internally, reinforcing the stale entry.
  5. No further PaneRemoved for pane 1 is ever emitted (pane already killed). Entry leaks for the lifetime of the ClientSession.

Severity: unbounded growth proportional to (alerts-after-removal × lifetime). Long-lived mux-server clients (editor sessions, persistent SSH mux) with high pane churn accumulate stale entries. Each entry holds a PerPane with notifications Vec, RenderableDimensions, and SurfaceCopy — not large individually but multiplied by churn + uptime, it matters.

Fix: expose a non-inserting accessor SessionHandler::per_pane_if_present(pane_id) -> Option<Arc<Mutex<PerPane>>> and use it for the Alert arm. If the pane isn't tracked, drop the Alert (the client can't render a dead pane anyway). Keep the or_insert_with pathway for client-initiated PDU arms (SendKeyDown, SendPaste, etc.) where insertion on first reference is correct. Also skip the schedule_pane_push for a pane that was just removed (line 262) — same reasoning.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:46:06.681760Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:49:18.407666Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:49:18.407372Z","close_reason":"Completed: added SessionHandler::per_pane_if_present non-inserting accessor and routed dispatch.rs Alert arm through it. -{"id":"ft-1memj.28","title":"[gui][test] GPU rendering tests and visual regression suite","description":"# GPU rendering tests and visual regression suite

## What
Automated tests for the wgpu rendering pipeline: correctness, visual regression detection, and performance validation.

## Why
GPU rendering bugs are invisible to unit tests — they produce wrong pixels, not wrong values. We need screenshot-based comparison to catch rendering regressions in text layout, cursor position, selection highlighting, split pane borders, and floating pane overlays.

## Tests

### Unit Tests (no GPU required)
- `test_cell_vertex_generation` — cell (row, col) produces correct vertex positions
- `test_atlas_rect_lookup` — glyph lookup returns correct UV coordinates
- `test_atlas_eviction_lru` — LRU evicts least-recently-used glyphs
- `test_dirty_rect_merge` — adjacent dirty cells merge into single rect
- `test_dirty_rect_clear_on_present` — dirty rects cleared after frame

### Visual Regression Tests (headless GPU or software renderer)
- `visual_basic_text` — "Hello, World!" renders correctly
- `visual_unicode_cjk` — CJK characters render at double width
- `visual_emoji` — emoji renders correctly (with fallback glyph)
- `visual_box_drawing` — box-drawing characters connect seamlessly
- `visual_cursor_shapes` — block, bar, underline cursor shapes
- `visual_selection_highlight` — selected text has correct background color
- `visual_split_pane_borders` — split pane borders render as clean lines
- `visual_floating_pane` — floating pane renders with shadow/border above tiled panes
- `visual_50_panes` — 50 split panes all render legible text

### Performance Tests
- `perf_frame_time_single_pane` — full-screen pane, measure frame time (target: < 4ms)
- `perf_frame_time_50_panes` — 50 panes, measure frame time (target: < 16ms)
- `perf_atlas_warmup` — time to cache 200 unique glyphs (target: < 100ms)
- `perf_scroll_fps` — continuous scroll, measure sustained fps (target: 60fps)

### E2E Script: `scripts/e2e_gpu_rendering.sh`
```
1. Launch frankenterm-gui in headless mode (--headless)
2. Open shell, run: echo "RENDER_TEST_$(date +%s)"
3. Capture screenshot to /tmp/ft_visual_test.png
4. Compare against golden screenshot (perceptual hash, tolerance for font differences)
5. Open 10 split panes, capture screenshot
6. Verify all 10 panes contain visible text (OCR or pixel sampling)
7. Measure frame time over 100 frames, report p50/p95/p99
8. Report: pass/fail with visual diff images on failure
```

## Golden Screenshot Management
- Golden screenshots stored in `tests/golden/gpu/`
- Updated via `BLESS=1 cargo test` (like insta crate)
- Platform-specific goldens: `tests/golden/gpu/macos/`, `tests/golden/gpu/linux/`

## Acceptance Criteria
- Visual regression suite runs in CI headlessly
- Frame time p99 < 16ms for single pane
- Font atlas handles full Unicode BMP without crash
- Visual diff images attached to failing CI runs for diagnosis","notes":"Reopened: stalled 13-22 days with no recent activity.  {"id":"ft-1p752","title":"[testing-fuzzing] crash-detector fuzz target for frankenterm-escape-parser Parser::parse raw bytes","description":"# Motivation

`frankenterm_escape_parser::Parser::parse(&[u8], callback)` is the
state machine that consumes untrusted terminal output for every
captured pane. The crate has 2 existing fuzz targets:

  * `termwiz_csi_parser.rs` — structure-aware around CSI sequences
  * `osc_marker_parser.rs`  — structure-aware around OSC markers

Neither is a plain raw-bytes crash detector. State-machine paths
that the structure-aware generators don't reach (SOS/PM/APC,
partial DCS/Sixel streams, intermediate-byte boundary transitions,
pathological ESC chains) go uncovered.

# Scope

New fuzz target `fuzz/fuzz_targets/escape_parser_raw.rs` that feeds
arbitrary bytes into `Parser::parse` with a no-op callback, asserts
no panic / no infinite loop / no allocator blow-up. Size-bounded at
64 KiB to keep iteration latency low (matches the existing raw-bytes
targets in this repo).

# Invariants (beyond crash-freedom)

1. `parse` consumes all bytes it's fed (state machine never strands).
2. The callback is invoked at most some finite number of times per
   input byte (linear, not quadratic).
3. `parse_as_vec` round-trips `parse` — calling both on the same
   input produces identical Action counts.

# Seed corpus

Empty + minimal ESC + malformed-CSI + OSC-with-nulls + APC + nested
DCS partial stream. 4-5 seeds.

# Build

    cargo +nightly fuzz run escape_parser_raw -- -runs=100000

Targets 2000+ exec/s given escape parsing is hot-path byte-driven

`crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs` pins envelope + schema conformance for exactly ONE tool: `wa.rules_list` (tests at lines 237-294, 313-346). `conformance_mcp_manifest.rs` pins the MANIFEST (tool schemas, additionalProperties=false, resource templates) but does NOT exercise the per-tool request → response envelope roundtrip.

`grep -oE '"wa\.[a-z_]+"' crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs | sort -u` → only `"wa.rules_list"`.

Out of 29 advertised `wa.*` tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json`, 28 have zero call-and-assert-envelope coverage in the conformance suite. The four core operator-facing tools are:

* `wa.search` — JSON envelope shape, hit schema, TOON parity, pane_filter/since_filter/until_filter/mode/metrics fields
* `wa.get_text` — pane_text map shape (ok/error variants), truncated + truncation_info edges
* `wa.send` — sender/response envelope (distinct from get_text), error codes for denied sends
* `wa.wait_for` — matched/elapsed_ms/polls/is_regex fields, timeout response envelope, rule_id match path

## Shape of fix

Mirror the `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` test pattern (lines 237-294 in mcp_conformance.rs):

1. spawn_client() → call_tool("wa.search", …)
2. parse_tool_envelope() → assert_success_envelope_shape() → assert_*_data_matches_documented_schema()
3. Also pin: invalid-format rejection, invalid-args hint text, TOON parity where applicable.

Existing helpers (`assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_invalid_args_envelope_shape`) already generalise — the new tests should reuse them verbatim.

## Why this matters

The MCP manifest conformance is the FREEZE on the advertised schema. Without per-tool envelope tests, a refactor can pass `conformance_mcp_manifest` (schema still looks right) while silently returning a non-conforming response body — e.g. field renamed in `ScannerHit` → clients break but tests stay green. ft-bs0ec froze this for `agent_inventory`; the four core operator tools deserve the same treatment.

## Scope guard

One new test file `mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs` (or additions to `mcp_conformance.rs`) with 4× (success + invalid-args) = 8 tests. ~250 LOC. No production changes.

## Explicitly out of scope

Mission / tx / events / cass families (separate bead if needed). Manifest tests (already covered).  {"id":"ft-qgk35","title":"[mor:security-engineering] wa.send trust boundary is 'which agent', not 'what bytes'","description":"# Observation

`WaSendTool` (mcp_tools.rs:1819) caps payload at 4 MiB (ft-05hfm) and
gates via the policy engine. Everything the authenticated agent
submits as \`text\` is then forwarded through
\`wezterm cli send-text -- <text>\` to the target pane.

The Command-args layering is safe from shell injection: args are
passed directly, not through \`sh -c\`. But send-text is typing
bytes into a terminal, and those bytes include the full control-char
space:

- \\x03 (Ctrl+C) kills the foreground process
- \\x04 (Ctrl+D) sends EOF, often exiting shells/REPLs
- \\x1a (Ctrl+Z) suspends
- Escape sequences can fake prompt text (`\\x1b[H\\x1b[2J$ `)
- Bracketed-paste wrappers can be forged

Trust model today: "if the agent is authenticated and policy allows
scope, arbitrary bytes are fine." This is appropriate for the
normal case (an agent typing \`npm test\` into its own pane). It is
less obviously appropriate for:

- agent A sending into agent B's pane (policy gates scope but not
  content)
- a compromised/misconfigured agent signing off its scope with a
  ghost Ctrl+C
- typing \`\\x1bc\` (reset terminal) into a neighbor pane to mask
  the audit trail of whatever just scrolled off

# Question

Would a lightweight content class — "does this payload contain
control characters outside the normal typing range?" — as a
separate policy capability be worth the complexity? The default
policy could allow control chars (current behavior); a hardening
toggle could require an explicit \`send-control-chars\` capability.

Agents that need to Ctrl+C their own long-running commands would
declare the capability up front; the 99% of typing-plain-text cases
would run without it and be structurally incapable of sending
Ctrl+C into another pane.

# Trust boundary

Current boundary: authenticated identity + policy scope + size cap.
Open question: should the boundary also include "which byte classes
can flow"? For security platforms, the answer is usually yes; the

 {"id":"ft-qgk35.1","title":"[security] wa.send: extend analyze_content_risk to flag terminal-control bytes","description":"# Parent

ft-qgk35 asked whether wa.send's trust boundary should include "what
bytes flow," not just "which agent is authenticated." Investigation
found the policy engine **already has content-aware gating** — the
question collapses from "should we add a mechanism?" to "should we
extend the existing mechanism to cover a specific blind spot?"

# Mechanism already in place

`PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk` (crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4792)
runs on every `ActionKind::SendText` with non-empty `command_text`.
It flags:

- **content.destructive_tokens** (40 pts): rm -rf, DROP TABLE, dd if=, …
- **content.sudo_elevation** (30 pts): sudo / doas / run0
- **content.multiline_complex** (15 pts): multi-line heredoc
- **content.pipe_chain** (10 pts): ≥ 2 pipes

And `PredicateKind::CommandPattern { patterns }`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:3296) lets operators write
regex-match rules on `command_text`.

So content-aware gating **exists**. The parent bead (ft-qgk35)'s
framing of "trust boundary is identity, not bytes" is incorrect —
the boundary already inspects bytes for a specific class of risks.

# The actual gap

`analyze_content_risk` is **shell-command aware** (rm -rf, sudo,
pipes) but not **terminal-control aware**. It does not flag:

- \x03 (Ctrl+C) — can kill another agent's foreground process
- \x04 (Ctrl+D) — can exit another agent's REPL/shell
- \x1a (Ctrl+Z) — can background another agent's work
- \x1b prefix (escape sequences) — can fake prompts, scramble
  the scrollback, clear the screen to mask evidence
- Bracketed-paste wrappers (\x1b[200~ ... \x1b[201~) — can be
  forged to make injected text read as human-pasted by agent
  detection heuristics

On the fleet of 200+ panes described in README.md, a compromised or
misconfigured agent that has scope to wa.send into a neighbor pane
today has unrestricted ability to type control bytes. The risk
surface is not hypothetical — agent A releasing its reservation on
pane B via a ghost Ctrl+C is a known class of coordination failure
in multi-agent terminal systems.

# Proposed tightening

Add a new risk factor to `analyze_content_risk`:

```rust
// Terminal-control bytes
let has_control_byte = text.bytes().any(|b|
    b == 0x03 || b == 0x04 || b == 0x1a  // Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D, Ctrl+Z
    || b == 0x1b                           // ESC (escape sequences)
);
if has_control_byte {
    self.add_factor(
        factors,
        "content.terminal_control_bytes",
        RiskCategory::Content,
        25,  // between multiline (15) and sudo (30)
        "Payload contains terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z or ESC)",
    );
}
```

Point value 25 chosen so default `risk_config.allow_max` still
permits typical interactive use (which should not contain control
bytes; agents that type `npm test` don't include \x03). Agents that
DO need Ctrl+C for long-running workflows trip the factor and
either:
  - pass through (if policy's allow_max accommodates 25), or
  - land in require-approval (if allow_max is below 25).

Either way the event is **visible in the audit trail** — which is
the actual ask from the parent bead.

# Alternative (higher-cost)

Introduce `PaneCapability::AcceptControlBytes` as a separate gate.
Richer but requires:
  - schema change to PaneCapabilities
  - migration for existing PaneState rows
  - config surface for operators to declare which panes accept
    control bytes

The risk-factor approach above is a ~10-line change with no schema
impact; the capability approach is ~150 lines and a migration. Pick
risk-factor unless a future bead explicitly wants per-pane
structural gating.

# Tests that should land with the fix

1. `analyze_content_risk_flags_ctrl_c`: "npm test\x03" triggers
   terminal_control_bytes factor.
2. `analyze_content_risk_plain_text_unchanged`: "npm test" does NOT
   trigger it (false-positive guard on printable ASCII).
3. `analyze_content_risk_flags_esc_prefix`: "\x1bc" (reset
   terminal) triggers.
4. `analyze_content_risk_flags_bracketed_paste_wrap`: the literal
   bytes `\x1b[200~...\x1b[201~` trigger (agent forging a paste).
5. `wa_send_dry_run_reports_control_byte_risk`: end-to-end from
   the MCP tool layer; dry-run with a Ctrl+C payload surfaces the
   factor in the injection preview.

# Disposition on parent

ft-qgk35 can be closed as `answered`: the mechanism exists; this
sub-bead tracks the specific extension. Keeping ft-qgk35 open risks
re-discovery of the same insight.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:15.998497Z","created_by":"content-trust-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:36:44.699744Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:36:44.698647Z","close_reason":"Verified in 010bac78","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","policy","security","wa-send"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-qgk35.1","depends_on_id":"ft-qgk35","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:15.998497Z","created_by":"content-trust-audit","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-qhyfh","title":"[LOW][review] user_packs discovery skips sandbox_resolve_dir (ft-6mxfs follow-up)","description":"# Observation

From a review pass over 10 swarm commits in the last 2 hours (git log --since='2 hours ago'), specifically the ft-6mxfs canonicalization fix (0239fbd6).

## Context

ft-6mxfs closed a directory-boundary symlink escape in `.ft/patterns` discovery by routing through `sandbox_resolve_dir(&ws_dir, root_path)` before walking. The fix is correct and well-tested.

## The gap

patterns.rs:938 — when `config.user_packs_enabled = true`, the code takes a different branch:

```rust
if config.user_packs_enabled {
    if let Some(user_dir) = config.resolved_user_packs_dir() {
        let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&user_dir)?;
        ...
    }
}
```

This call does NOT route through `sandbox_resolve_dir`. The user-packs dir is either the user-configured `user_packs_dir` (tilde-expanded) or `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/patterns`.

## Threat-model delta

Unlike `.ft/patterns` (inside a cloned repo — attacker-controlled), `~/.config/wa/patterns/` is under the user's own control. So the escape window is narrower — the user would have to set up the symlink themselves, at which point they already have write access.

However:
- If the user shares dotfiles across machines and a symlink in their config dir points at something sensitive on one machine but not another, a stray `wa` invocation could read files outside the intended scope.
- The inner per-file `sandbox_resolve` (ft-fbee5) still provides a per-YAML defense, so this is defense-in-depth, not a primary vulnerability.

## Proposed fix

Call `sandbox_resolve_dir(&user_dir, &user_dir.parent().unwrap_or(&user_dir))` or pass an explicit `user_sandbox_root` so the canonicalized-inside-root invariant extends to the user-packs path. Keep the behavior that a missing directory is Ok(None) (already the common case).

## Review context

Nine transactional tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json` have manifest-freeze coverage (schema gate, additionalProperties=false) but no envelope roundtrip conformance test:

* `wa.mission_state` — MissionRunState enum serialization, agent/action assignment maps
* `wa.mission_abort` — abort outcome envelope, idempotency on already-aborted missions
* `wa.mission_explain` — explain payload shape, trace graph
* `wa.mission_pause` / `wa.mission_resume` — lifecycle state-machine transitions
* `wa.tx_plan` — MissionTxContract shape, precondition list, classification field
* `wa.tx_run` — phase state transitions, compensating action record
* `wa.tx_show` — include_contract flag behaviour, chain verification data
* `wa.tx_rollback` — rollback outcome record

## Fix shape

Follow the same pattern as `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` tests at mcp_conformance.rs:237-294 + the `agent_inventory_contract.json` golden shipped under ft-bs0ec (97ec6350). Recommended layout:

1. Fixture JSON per tool under `tests/fixtures/golden_robot_envelope/wa_{tool}.json` — canonical success envelope plus one denial/invalid-args envelope.
2. Test module `mcp_conformance_mission_tx.rs` that calls each tool with deterministic seeded inputs, canonicalises the response (BTreeMap ordering, strip volatile timestamps), and golden-compares.
3. Reuse helpers: `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, plus `UPDATE_GOLDEN=1` regen wiring from `agent_inventory_golden.rs`.

## Why this matters

Mission + Tx are the load-bearing transactional surface (precondition check, rollback, chain verification). A silent envelope drift here corrupts downstream client decision paths worse than a UI-level surface drift would — the transactional semantics are what callers depend on to unwind on failure. The mission_contract_golden.rs at line 80589845 pins MissionTxContract serde roundtrip but NOT the MCP envelope that wraps it.

## Scope guard

9 goldens + 9 tests + regen harness. ~500 LOC. No production changes unless drift is found during fixture capture — in which case a separate bug bead would be filed per drift.

## Explicitly out of scope

Core operator tools (wa.search/get_text/send/wait_for) — tracked under the separate core-tools conformance bead.  {"id":"ft-qyw1z","title":"[review][CRITICAL] mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs WIP uses std::env::set_var, violates forbid(unsafe_code) workspace lint","description":"Uncommitted WIP expansion of crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs (pane 2, ft-4o5mb success-path harness) calls std::env::set_var and std::env::remove_var 6 times across FakeWezterm::install and EnvGuard::Drop.

Rust 2024 edition made std::env::set_var/remove_var unsafe fn. The workspace declares [workspace.lints.rust] unsafe_code = "forbid" (Cargo.toml), so this test WILL NOT COMPILE once committed — the harness currently builds only because the uncommitted diff has not been fed through cargo check --all-targets.

Evidence:
- git diff crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs: 6 set_var/remove_var call sites in FakeWezterm::install() and impl Drop for EnvGuard.
- Cargo.toml workspace lints: unsafe_code = "forbid".
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/lib.rs:44: #![forbid(unsafe_code)] (reinforced at crate level).
- MEMORY.md already flags this constraint: 'std::env::set_var is unsafe in Rust 2024 edition — use Command::env() instead'.

Fix options (pick one, no new beads needed):
1. Drop the FakeWezterm env-var approach. Instead, pass FT_WEZTERM_CLI/FT_FAKE_WEZTERM_STATE_DIR via a builder API that threads explicit config into the MCP server (no process-global mutation).
2. Spawn the MCP server in a subprocess with Command::env("FT_WEZTERM_CLI", ...) and talk to it over stdio — Command::env is safe and the workspace policy already endorses this pattern.
3. Convert the integration test to a #[test] that uses a hand-rolled test harness struct carrying the CLI path + state dir through Config, not env vars.
